summary: Ancient Greek tragedian (~525–456 BCE), the 'father of tragedy.' Most commonly identified by the Oresteia trilogy (Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, The Eumenides), Prometheus Bound, The Persians, Seven Against Thebes, and The Suppliant Women. Defeats Euripides in a poetry contest in Aristophanes' The Frogs. Apocryphally died when an eagle dropped a turtle on his bald head.

works.name: Oresteia (Agamemnon, The Libation Bearers, The Eumenides)
works.description: Trilogy about the murder of Agamemnon by Clytemnestra, Orestes's revenge, and his trial and acquittal.

clues.clue: Agamemnon: Clytemnestra lays out purple carpet for Agamemnon, then murders him (revenge for sacrifice of Iphigenia); Aegisthus helps
examples: a purple carpet is laid out for the title character, who is murdered by Aegisthus and Clytemnestra as revenge for the sacrifice of Iphigenia
a woman imagines herself tangled in a net and cries out that a house stinks of murder

clues.clue: Agamemnon: Cassandra enters praying for revenge on captors; at start cries house 'stinks of murder' (tangled in a net)
examples: a woman imagines herself tangled in a net and cries out that a house stinks of murder, but still enters while praying for revenge on her captors

clues.clue: The Libation Bearers: Electra finds a lock of hair and footprints resembling her own at a tomb left by Orestes; two characters disguise as travelers from Phocis
examples: a young woman sees a set of footprints that resemble her own and discovers a lock of hair on a tomb left by her brother
two characters disguise themselves as travellers from Phocis

clues.clue: The Eumenides: Athena breaks tie to acquit Orestes at the Areopagus; argues father is more important parent since Athena sprang from Zeus's head; Furies renamed at the end
examples: it is argued that the father is the more important parent, because Athena sprang from Zeus' head without a mother, leading to the acquittal of the protagonist
The invention of the jury trial is central to the Eumenides

clues.clue: Furies pursue Orestes for killing his mother Clytemnestra; they get their name changed to 'Eumenides' at the end
examples: chased by the three Furies, who get their name changed at the end

works.name: Prometheus Bound
works.description: Play about Prometheus chained to a mountain by Zeus for giving fire to mankind.

clues.clue: Prometheus chained to a mountain by Kratos and Bia; struck by lightning; liver eaten; tells Oceanids about marriage threatening Zeus's downfall
examples: gets struck by lightning and has his liver eaten while he is chained to a mountain
telling the Oceanids about a marriage that could lead to Zeus's downfall after being trapped by Kratos and Bia

clues.clue: Opens 'O divine air Breezes on swift bird-wings... The multitudinous laughter of Mother Earth!'
examples: title character's first words are a speech that begins, 'O divine air Breezes on swift bird-wings... The multitudinous laughter of Mother Earth!'

clues.clue: Martin West and Mark Griffith argue it was actually composed by Aeschylus's son Euphorion
examples: Martin West and Mark Griffith have argued that one of this man's attributed works was in fact composed by his son, Euphorion

works.name: The Persians
works.description: Earliest surviving play. About the Persian reaction to their defeat at Salamis. Unique for its contemporary subject.

clues.clue: Earliest surviving play; Atossa mourns defeat of her son (Xerxes); messenger repeats a Greek battle cry; unique for contemporary situation; Pericles was choregos
examples: sees a messenger repeat a Greek battle cry, after which Atossa mourns the defeat of her son; that is the earliest surviving play
surprising for eliciting sympathy in its audience for its titular characters, the Persians

clues.clue: Atossa dreams of two beautiful women fighting before her son yokes them to his chariot; told by husband's shade to put on finest clothes
examples: a woman has a dream of two beautiful women who begin to fight before her son yokes them to his chariot

works.name: Seven Against Thebes
works.description: Play about the war between Eteocles and Polynices at Thebes.

clues.clue: Spy tells antagonist about the title characters' alliance against Eteocles at Argos
examples: The antagonist is told by his Spy of the title characters at Argos' camp

works.name: The Suppliant Women
works.description: Play about the Danaids fleeing marriage to the sons of Aegyptus.

clues.clue: Chorus (the Danaids) pray to Zeus for protection from the lust of young men; gadfly's flight to the Nile described
examples: the flight of a gadfly to the Nile is described by the chorus in a prayer to Zeus asking for protection from the lust of young men

clues.clue: Defeats Euripides in a poetry contest in Aristophanes' The Frogs; uses 'lost his little oil flask' to interrupt Euripides' prologues
examples: He is praised as teaching his audiences to seek victory in battle
uses the phrase 'lost his little oil flask' to interrupt another character

clues.clue: Apocryphally died when an eagle dropped a turtle on his bald head
examples: apocryphally died when an eagle dropped a turtle on his bald head

clues.clue: One character asks to speak with master instead of mistress as 'men do not mince words'
examples: asks to speak with the master of a house instead of the mistress, as men do not mince words

comprehensive_summary:
Aeschylus (~525–456 BCE) was an ancient Greek tragedian, often called the 'father of tragedy.' He is most commonly identified by the Oresteia trilogy, Prometheus Bound, The Persians, Seven Against Thebes, and The Suppliant Women.

The Oresteia consists of three plays. In Agamemnon, Clytemnestra lays out a purple carpet for the returning king and murders him (with Aegisthus's help) in revenge for his sacrifice of Iphigenia. Cassandra enters the house praying for revenge, crying that it 'stinks of murder.' In The Libation Bearers, Electra finds a lock of hair and footprints resembling her own at her father's tomb; Orestes and Pylades disguise themselves as travelers from Phocis to carry out their revenge on Clytemnestra. In The Eumenides, the Furies pursue Orestes for matricide. Athena breaks a tie to acquit him at the Areopagus, arguing the father is the more important parent since she herself sprang from Zeus's head. The Furies are renamed 'Eumenides' ('Kindly Ones') at the end. This play centers on the invention of the jury trial.

Prometheus Bound depicts Prometheus chained to a mountain by Kratos and Bia, struck by lightning, and having his liver eaten. He tells the Oceanids about a marriage that could threaten Zeus's downfall. It opens 'O divine air Breezes on swift bird-wings... The multitudinous laughter of Mother Earth!' Some scholars (Martin West, Mark Griffith) argue it was composed by Aeschylus's son Euphorion.

The Persians is the earliest surviving play, uniquely set in contemporary events: Atossa mourns the defeat of her son Xerxes at Salamis. Pericles served as choregos. Atossa dreams of two women fighting before her son yokes them to his chariot.

In Aristophanes' The Frogs, Aeschylus defeats Euripides in a poetry contest, using the phrase 'lost his little oil flask' to interrupt Euripides' prologues. Aeschylus apocryphally died when an eagle dropped a turtle on his bald head.
